title: Josephן¿½s Dreams And Reubenן¿½s Brethren (A Recital In Six Chapters)
content:
CHAPTER I

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I cannot blame old Israel yet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I am not a sage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shall not know until I get
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The son of my old age.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mysteries of this Vale of Tears
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We will perchance explain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When we have lived a thousand years
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And died and come again.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No doubt old Jacob acted mean
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Towards his fatherן¿½s son;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But other hands were none too clean,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When all is said and done.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were some things that had to be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In those old days, ן¿½tis true,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But with old Jacobן¿½s history
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This tale has nought to do.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (They had to keep the birth-rate up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And populate the land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They did it, too, by simple means
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That we canן¿½t understand.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Patriarchsן¿½ way of fixing things
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would make an awful row,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Sarahן¿½s plain, straightforward plan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would never answer now.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This is a tale of simple men
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And one precocious boy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A spoilt kid, and, as usual,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His fatherן¿½s hope and joy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (It mostly is the way in which
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The younger sons behave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That brings the old manן¿½s grey hairs down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In sorrow to the grave.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Jacob loved the whelp, and made,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While meaning to be kind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A coat of many colours that
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would strike a nigger blind!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It struck the brethren green, ן¿½twas said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d take a pinch of salt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their coats had coloured patches too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that was not their fault.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young Joseph had a soft thing on,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, humbugged from his birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You may depend he worked the thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For all that it was worth.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And that he grafted not but crowed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You donן¿½t need to be told,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he was mighty cocky, with
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His ן¿½Lo!ן¿½ and his ן¿½Behold!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He took in all his brothers said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And went and told his Dad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then, when someone split on him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No wonder they were mad.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But still he wasnן¿½t satisfied,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And it would almost seem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He itched to rile his brethren, for
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He went and dreamed a dream,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And told it to his brothers straight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (So Genesis believes):,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lo! we were working in the field,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we were binding sheaves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And my sheaf rose and stood upright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, straightway, for a sign,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your sheaves came round about and made
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Obeisance to mine!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brethren stared and made comment
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In words that were not mild,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when the meaning dawned on them
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You bet that they were wild!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Joseph left those angry men
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To boil and blow off steam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ambled, chuckling, home agen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To dream another dream.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Behold! Iן¿½ve dreamed a dream once more!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He told ן¿½em, frank and free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The sun, moon, and eleven stars
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have likewise bowed to me!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Perhaps Astronomy has changed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since Joseph saw the light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I have wondered what the sun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was doing out at night.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when they dropped!, you never heard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In sheds or shanty bars,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such awful language as escaped
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From those eleven stars.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know how Jacob-Israel loved
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His hopeful youngest pup;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, when he heard the latest dream,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It shook the old man up.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Joseph talked his father round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who humoured every whim
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Perhaps old Jacob half-believed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They would bow down to him):
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, anyway, as always was,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He backed the youngest son,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sent the others with the sheep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out to the Check-ן¿½em run.



CHAPTER II

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now Jacob, with that wondrous tact
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That doting parents show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, anxious for his sons out back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sent, of all others, Joe!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see if it was well with them
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And they were not asleep),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With one eye on his brothersן¿½ camp,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And one eye on the sheep.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He drew a blank on Check-ן¿½em run,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Got bushed, too, youן¿½ll be bound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A certain cove, thereן¿½s always one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw Joseph mooning round.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He asked him how it came to pass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what it was about,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And said, ן¿½Theyן¿½re trav-linן¿½ now for grass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Doothen, further out.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He also muttered, ן¿½Strike me blue!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While staring at the clothes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d never seen a jackaroo
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With such a coat as Joeן¿½s.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He set the nameless on the track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And scratched his head to think,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But gave it best, and, riding back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said firmly, ן¿½Strike me pink!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas blazing hot in Doothen then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sweat ran down in streams,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It melted out the memory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of even Josephן¿½s dreams!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d had some trouble with the sheep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some Arabs and a ן¿½shirkן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was a favourable time
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Joe to get to work.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They saw him coming, ן¿½afar offן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In this case, you might note,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their eyesight wasnן¿½t wonderful,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Considering the coat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what with sheep, and dust, and flies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And damned shirks in the swim
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With sheep stealers, the brethren were
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For absenteeing him.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, add to that, he scared the kine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his infernal coat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They trampled on the sheep and swine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And startled every goat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brethren had to round up then
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As fast as ass could go,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when they got to camp agen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d fixed it up for Joe.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save poor old Rube, he had the blight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, grafting all the same,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He only looked on family rows
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As just a blooming shame.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like many an easy-going man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had a cunning soul.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He said, ן¿½We will not kill the kid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But shove him in a hole,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leave him there to dream oן¿½ thingsן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s not the slightest doubt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He meant to slip round after dark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pull the youngster out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fill his gourd and tucker-bag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tell him ן¿½Not to mindן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And start him on the back-track with
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A gentle kick behind.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some ן¿½Tothersider prospectors
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had been there poking round;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You may depend that Reuben knew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas ן¿½dry and shallow groundן¿½.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They dropped young Joseph in a hole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The giddy little goat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left him there, to cool his heels,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Without his overcoat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Donן¿½t think that Moses, such a whale
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On dry facts, thought it wet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To say, when theyן¿½d chucked Joseph in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was an empty pit!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So many things are preached and said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½er the Bible is
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To prove that Moses never read
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½proofsן¿½ of Genesis.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But letן¿½s get on. While having grub,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A brethren sniffed and ן¿½seenן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some Ishmaelites pass through the scrub,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or O-asses, I mean.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d been right out to Gilead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A rather longish trip,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For camel-loads of balm, and myrrh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And spicery for ן¿½Gyp.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Iן¿½ve often seen the Afghans pass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With camel strings out back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thought ן¿½twas somewhat similar
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On that old Bible track.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I donן¿½t know much of balm and myrrh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatever they may be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But eן¿½en when sheepskins were not there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve smelt the spicery.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was the same in Canaan then
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As it is here to-day:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A sudden thought jerked Judah up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ן¿½brofit ן¿½ straight away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brethren got on one end too
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Judah jumped and said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weן¿½ll sell the kid for what he brings!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s no good when heן¿½s dead.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, to be short, they being Jews,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½chosingן¿½ of the earth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They sold him to the Ishmaelites
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For more than twice his worth.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Some Midianitish auctioneers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were also on the job.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas ן¿½twenty bits of silverן¿½, which
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I sן¿½pose was twenty bob.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they most comfortably got
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young Joseph off their hands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Ishmael never bothered much
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About receipts or brands.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (They spake not of his dreams and cheek,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His laziness, or ן¿½skiteן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No doubt they thought the Ishmaelites
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would see to that all right.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Reuben came; heן¿½d been around
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To watch the sheep a bit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on his way back to the camp
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He slipped round by the pit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To give young Joe a drink. He stared,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, thinking Joe was dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He rent his gown like mad, and ran
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ashes for his head.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (As if that would do any good!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I only know that I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cannot afford to rend my clothes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When my relations die.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I donן¿½t suppose they would come back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or that the world would care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I went howling for a year
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With ashes in my hair.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You say he counted on a new
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rig-out? Yes? And you know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Jacob tore his garment too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So that old cock wonן¿½t crow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look here! You keep your smart remarks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till after I am gone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wonן¿½t have Reuben silver-tailed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor Pharaoh, later on.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brethren humbugged Reuben well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For fear heן¿½d take the track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sneak in on the Ishmaelites,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And steal young Joseph back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or fight it out if he was caught,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And die, as it might be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, at the best, go down with Joe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And into slavery.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young Simeon slipped into the scrub,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To where the coat was hid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Judah stayed and wept with Rube,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Levi killed a kid.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they fixed up the wild-beast yarn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Hebrews sadly note,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Considering the price of cloth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They had to spoil the coat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (There was a yam about old Rube
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That all true men despise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spread by his fatherן¿½s concubines,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A vicious strumpetן¿½s lies.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I believe old Moses was,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As we are, well aware
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Reuben stood in this last scene
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The central figure there.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I feel for poor old Israelן¿½s grief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Believing all the same
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And not with atheist unbelief)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Jacob was to blame.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas ever so, and shall be done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While one fond fool has breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fond folly drives the youngest son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ruin and to death.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The caravan went jogging on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Pharaohן¿½s royal town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Genesis gives no account
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Josephן¿½s journey down.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wouldnן¿½t be surprised to hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He found it pretty rough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thereן¿½s a bare chance that his hide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As well as cheek, was tough.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I see them toiling through the heat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In patches and in dirt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With sand-grooved sandals on their feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And slaves without a shirt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dust-caked thirst, the burning ground,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mad and maddening flies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That gathered like black goggles round
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The piccaninniesן¿½ eyes.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Ishmaelites had tempers brief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And whips of hide and gut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sometimes, pן¿½raps, for Hagarן¿½s sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gave Joe an extra cut.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When, fainting by the way, he felt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stimulating touch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have no doubt he often wished
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He hadnן¿½t dreamed so much.



ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He didnן¿½t dream much on that trip,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Although he thought a lot.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ However, they got down to ן¿½Gyp
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In good time, where he got
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wash and rest, he needed both,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in the old slave-yard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was sold to Captain Potiphar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Pharaohן¿½s body-guard.



INTERLUDE

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I pause to state that later on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And it seems worth the halt)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Smart Judah gat into a mess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though it was not his fault.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I would only like to say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In this most thankless task,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wives sell to husbands every day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And that without a mask.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, what with family rows and drought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blessed women too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fathers of terrestrial tribes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had quite enough to do.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They had to graft both day and night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With no rest, save the last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For when they were not grafting they
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were populating fast.



CHAPTER III

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Captain was a casual man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But seemed a shrewd one too;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He got young Josephן¿½s measure soon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saw what he could do.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Lord was with Joe, Moses said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know that Joe had pluck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I believe ן¿½twas mostly check,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his infernal luck.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Captain made him manager,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Housekeeper, overseer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And found that this arrangement paid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That much at least is clear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what with merchants, clerks, and slaves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Joe led a busy life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With one eye on the maid-servants,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Jeamesן¿½ and Pottyן¿½s wife.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Captain seemed a casual man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½ן¿½Gypן¿½ was on the glide:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a growing tendency
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To live and let things slide.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He left all things in Josephן¿½s hands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ According to old Mose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And knew not what he had besides
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His tucker and his clothes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I guess he had a shrewd idea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For it is now, as then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The world most often makes mistakes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With easy-going men.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Captain often went away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For quietness and rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, maybe, for some other things,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, Potiphar knew best.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps the missus knew it too,
